text: The surgical team begins the ventral hernia repair by widely draping the patient to accommodate a three-port approach. A blunt tip trocar is inserted in the right subcostal area under direct laparoscopic visualization. Tenaculums are used on the skin for counter traction as the trocar is wiggled into the peritoneum. Carbon dioxide is insufflated to create space, allowing the trocar to be fully inserted, establishing one of the robotic arms with a twelve millimeter standard laparoscopic port.

The patient is positioned supine, and the laparoscopic camera port is placed laterally to provide ample space, avoiding arm clashing. A long trocar is inserted in the lower right abdomen for the robotic arm attachment, suitable for larger patients. The camera port is positioned with more than ten centimeters of clearance to the third port, where the robotic arm will be placed.

The surgeon positions the remote center into the muscle layer and prepares to dock the robotic system. The robot is directed straight in, and the camera arm is attached to the camera trocar using a long trocar for ample grip. The number one robotic port site is designated for the right-hand dissection port, utilizing a standard robotic eight and a half millimeter trocar.

The surgeon inserts a vessel sealer into the first robotic arm for dissection, using a 30-degree laparoscope to view the abdominal wall. Adhesions are visible, and the fenestrated bipolar instrument is prepared in the second robotic arm for grasping the peritoneum.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to dissect and free the abdominal wall from adhesions, preparing the area for ventral hernia repair. The instrument's low grip strength allows atraumatic manipulation of the bowel, while its bipolar capability controls any oozing from fatty tissues. The median umbilical ligaments in the lower abdomen are moved aside to secure the mesh directly to the fascia.

The surgeon uses an advanced bipolar device with a blunt tip to dissect and coagulate tissue, freeing the fascia to expose the fascial defect. The goal is to close the defect primarily and place mesh.
The surgeon continues to clear the fascial edges of fatty tissue and reduces the omentum that is incarcerated in the hernia, preparing for a laparoscopic underlay mesh technique.

The surgeon uses blunt and sharp dissection to release the omentum incarcerated in the hernia, located near the base of the falciform ligament extending to the umbilical area. A nine-centimeter round Paritex polyester mesh with a collagen adhesion barrier is prepared for placement after primarily closing the defect, with options to sew, tack, or use trans-fascial sutures for securing the mesh.
The surgeon strips the peritoneum back to expose the fascia, preparing to place the mesh directly onto the fascia for secure incorporation.

The surgeon uses a relatively large needle to take a generous bite of the fascia, approximately two centimeters, starting inferior to the apex of the defect. A V-lock suture is employed, allowing the creation of a secure closure without traditional knots by threading the suture through an eye on the tail end and doubling back for friction. The suture is long, so multiple throws are placed before tightening, allowing carbon dioxide to escape and the abdominal wall to relax, facilitating the closure of the fascial defect.

The surgeon places multiple suture passes through the fascial defect, using a running suture technique to achieve a tight and complete closure. The process involves careful attention to visual cues, such as the tension of the suture and the bending of instruments, to ensure the fascia is pulled together securely. The surgeon releases some carbon dioxide to relax the abdominal wall, facilitating the closure.

The surgeon uses a needle holder with a scissor at the back of the jaw to cut the suture at the end of the suture line. Care is taken not to accidentally cut the suture while advancing it. The surgeon ensures visibility of the fascial edges for the final suture throws, reaching the apex of the defect and extending slightly beyond to secure the closure.

The surgeon parks the needle and releases some carbon dioxide to relax the abdominal wall, then continues to tighten the suture line, effectively closing the fascial defect. Instead of tying a knot, the suture line is backed up on itself to secure the closure.
The surgeon backs up the suture line by two or three passes to create enough friction for the suture to lock securely.

The surgeon prepares to place a parietex polyester mesh with a collagen adhesion barrier over the fascial defect. The mesh is rolled with the adhesion barrier inside to prevent peeling. Using a large needle holder, the surgeon positions the mesh, ensuring it covers the primary suture line. Trans-fascial sutures will secure the mesh against the abdominal wall, initially held manually until the stitches are placed.

The surgeon uses robotic instruments to sew around a complete 360-degree circle, maneuvering needle holders in various directions to achieve a secure closure. The robotic technique allows for dexterous handling with both hands, facilitating efficient suturing from multiple angles.

The surgeon nears the end of the current suture line and prepares to start a new one, overlapping the suture lines slightly without tying a knot. A 2-0 suture with a smaller needle is used to secure the mesh, ensuring good full bites on the fascia for a strong repair.

The surgeon crosses over the existing suture line with a few additional throws to relieve tension, ensuring there is no knot at the end. The repair is shown with a primary closure covered by a mesh underlay, completely sewn in with non-absorbable suture. The mesh provides good coverage and secures the closure, with continuous sutures around the edges to prevent gapping.
